packs.name: Elasticsearch
packs.description: Protects against destructive Elasticsearch REST API operations like index deletion, delete-by-query, index close, and cluster setting changes.

structural_patterns.name: es-curl-delete-doc
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against /_doc deletes a document from Elasticsearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a document removes it from the index. The document ID becomes available for reuse. Applications expecting this document will receive 404 errors. Search results will no longer include this document.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the document first to verify
- Use document versioning for conflict detection
- Index a tombstone document instead of deleting

structural_patterns.name: es-curl-delete-by-query
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X POST to _delete_by_query deletes documents matching the query.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Delete-by-query removes all documents matching the query criteria. A malformed query can delete far more documents than intended. The operation runs asynchronously and cannot be easily cancelled once started.

Safer alternatives:
- Run the query with _search first to preview matches
- Use scroll_size to limit batch size
- Set wait_for_completion=false and monitor progress

structural_patterns.name: es-curl-close-index
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X POST to _close closes an index, making it unavailable for reads/writes.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Closing an index blocks all read and write operations. Applications will receive errors when accessing closed indices. Closed indices still consume disk space but release memory. Use _all with extreme caution.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /_cat/indices to review indices first
- Close specific indices rather than patterns
- Use index lifecycle management for automated handling

structural_patterns.name: es-curl-delete-index
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against an Elasticsearch index (or _all/*) deletes data permanently.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an index permanently removes all documents, mappings, and settings. Using _all or * wildcards can delete multiple indices at once. This operation is immediate and irreversible. Data cannot be recovered without snapshots.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /_cat/indices to review before deletion
- Create a snapshot before deleting
- Disable action.destructive_requires_name for safety

structural_patterns.name: es-curl-cluster-settings
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X PUT to /_cluster/settings changes cluster settings and can be dangerous.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Cluster settings affect all nodes and can impact stability, performance, and data integrity. Persistent settings survive restarts. Changes to allocation, recovery, or shard settings can trigger data movement.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /_cluster/settings to review current values
- Use transient settings for testing
- Test changes on non-production clusters first

structural_patterns.name: es-http-delete-doc
structural_patterns.reason: http DELETE against /_doc deletes a document from Elasticsearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a document removes it from the index. The document ID becomes available for reuse. Applications expecting this document will receive 404 errors. Search results will no longer include this document.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the document first to verify
- Use document versioning for conflict detection
- Index a tombstone document instead of deleting

structural_patterns.reason: http PUT to /_cluster/settings changes cluster settings and can be dangerous.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Cluster settings affect all nodes and can impact stability, performance, and data integrity. Persistent settings survive restarts. Changes to allocation, recovery, or shard settings can trigger data movement.

Safer alternatives:

packs.name: OpenSearch
packs.description: Protects against destructive OpenSearch REST API operations and AWS CLI domain deletions.

structural_patterns.name: os-curl-delete-doc
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against /_doc deletes a document from OpenSearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting individual documents removes them from search results immediately. While less destructive than bulk operations, this can affect applications expecting the document to exist.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the document first to verify content
- Use _update API to mark as deleted instead
- Ensure document ID is correct before deletion

structural_patterns.name: os-curl-delete-by-query
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X POST to _delete_by_query deletes documents matching the query.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The _delete_by_query endpoint can delete large numbers of documents at once. A poorly constructed query can remove more data than intended. This operation cannot be easily undone.

Safer alternatives:
- Use _search with same query first to preview matches
- Add conflicts=abort to stop on version conflicts
- Use scroll_size to process in smaller batches

structural_patterns.name: os-curl-close-index
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X POST to _close closes an index, making it unavailable for reads/writes.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Closing an index makes it completely unavailable for search and indexing. Applications will receive errors until the index is reopened. Using _all or * can close all indices at once.

Safer alternatives:
- Check index status with _cat/indices first
- Use specific index names instead of wildcards
- Verify no active applications depend on the index

structural_patterns.name: os-curl-delete-index
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against an OpenSearch index (or _all/*) deletes data permanently.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an OpenSearch index permanently removes all documents, mappings, and settings. Using _all or * patterns can delete multiple indices at once. This action cannot be undone without restoring from a snapshot.

Safer alternatives:
- _cat/indices: List indices before deletion
- Create a snapshot before deleting
- Close the index first if just archiving

structural_patterns.name: os-http-delete-doc
structural_patterns.reason: http DELETE against /_doc deletes a document from OpenSearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting individual documents removes them from search results immediately. While less destructive than bulk operations, this can affect applications expecting the document to exist.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the document first to verify content
- Use _update API to mark as deleted instead
- Ensure document ID is correct before deletion

structural_patterns.name: os-http-delete-by-query
structural_patterns.reason: http POST to _delete_by_query deletes documents matching the query.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The _delete_by_query endpoint can delete large numbers of documents at once. A poorly constructed query can remove more data than intended. This operation cannot be easily undone.

Safer alternatives:
- Use _search with same query first to preview matches
- Add conflicts=abort to stop on version conflicts
- Use scroll_size to process in smaller batches

structural_patterns.name: os-http-close-index
structural_patterns.reason: http POST to _close closes an index, making it unavailable for reads/writes.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Closing an index makes it completely unavailable for search and indexing. Applications will receive errors until the index is reopened. Using _all or * can close all indices at once.

Safer alternatives:
- Check index status with _cat/indices first
- Use specific index names instead of wildcards
- Verify no active applications depend on the index

structural_patterns.name: os-http-delete-index
structural_patterns.reason: http DELETE against an OpenSearch index (or _all/*) deletes data permanently.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an OpenSearch index permanently removes all documents, mappings, and settings. Using _all or * patterns can delete multiple indices at once. This action cannot be undone without restoring from a snapshot.

Safer alternatives:
- _cat/indices: List indices before deletion
- Create a snapshot before deleting
- Close the index first if just archiving

structural_patterns.name: aws-opensearch-delete-domain
structural_patterns.reason: aws opensearch delete-domain permanently deletes an OpenSearch domain.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an OpenSearch domain removes the entire cluster including all indices, data, and configuration. This is irreversible and affects all applications using the domain. Automated snapshots may also be deleted.

Safer alternatives:
- aws opensearch describe-domain: Review domain configuration
- Create a manual snapshot before deletion
- Export data to S3 for long-term retention

structural_patterns.name: aws-opensearch-delete-inbound-connection
structural_patterns.reason: aws opensearch delete-inbound-connection removes an OpenSearch connection.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an inbound connection removes cross-cluster connectivity. Applications performing cross-cluster searches or replication will fail. The connection must be re-established from the source domain.

Safer alternatives:
- List connections to understand dependencies
- Verify no active cross-cluster operations
- Update applications to use alternative data sources

structural_patterns.name: aws-opensearch-delete-outbound-connection
structural_patterns.reason: aws opensearch delete-outbound-connection removes an OpenSearch connection.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an outbound connection removes cross-cluster connectivity from this domain. Any cross-cluster searches or replication to other domains will fail. Re-establishing requires a new connection request.

Safer alternatives:
- List connections to understand what will be affected
- Coordinate with teams using connected domains
- Test connection removal in a non-production environment

structural_patterns.name: aws-opensearch-delete-package
structural_patterns.reason: aws opensearch delete-package removes an OpenSearch package.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a package removes custom plugins, dictionaries, or scripts. Domains using this package may experience errors or degraded functionality. The package must be re-uploaded if needed again.

Safer alternatives:
- List domains using this package first
- Dissociate package from domains before deletion
- Keep a copy of package contents for recovery

packs.name: Algolia
packs.description: Protects against destructive Algolia operations like deleting indices, clearing objects, removing rules/synonyms, and deleting API keys.

structural_patterns.name: algolia-indices-delete
structural_patterns.reason: algolia indices delete permanently removes an Algolia index.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an Algolia index permanently removes all searchable records, settings, rules, and synonyms. Search functionality for applications using this index will fail immediately. Re-indexing may require significant time and resources.

Safer alternatives:
- algolia indices list: Review indices before deletion
- Export index data using algolia indices browse
- Create a replica or copy before deleting

structural_patterns.name: algolia-indices-clear
structural_patterns.reason: algolia indices clear removes all objects from an Algolia index.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Clearing an Algolia index removes all searchable records while preserving settings, rules, and synonyms. Search will return no results until data is re-indexed. This is irreversible without re-indexing from source data.

Safer alternatives:
- Export objects using algolia indices browse first
- Use a staging index for testing
- Delete specific objects instead of clearing all

structural_patterns.name: algolia-rules-delete
structural_patterns.reason: algolia rules delete removes index rules.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting Algolia rules removes search customizations like query rewrites, promotions, and filters. Search behavior may change unexpectedly for users relying on these rules for relevant results.

Safer alternatives:
- algolia rules browse: Export rules before deletion
- Disable rules temporarily instead of deleting
- Test rule changes on a replica index first

structural_patterns.name: algolia-synonyms-delete
structural_patterns.reason: algolia synonyms delete removes synonym entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting Algolia synonyms removes word associations that improve search matching. Users searching with alternative terms may no longer find expected results. Synonym configurations can take time to rebuild.

Safer alternatives:
- algolia synonyms browse: Export synonyms before deletion
- Test synonym changes on a replica index
- Document synonym configurations for recovery

structural_patterns.name: algolia-apikeys-delete
structural_patterns.reason: algolia apikeys delete removes API keys and can break integrations.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an Algolia API key immediately revokes access for all applications using it. Search and indexing operations will fail with authentication errors. The key cannot be recovered after deletion.

Safer alternatives:
- algolia apikeys list: Review keys and their usage
- Create new API keys before deleting old ones
- Rotate keys by updating applications first

structural_patterns.name: algolia-sdk-delete-index
structural_patterns.reason: Algolia SDK deleteIndex removes an index.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The deleteIndex SDK method permanently removes an Algolia index and all its contents. This includes all records, settings, rules, and synonyms. Production search functionality will fail immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- Use browseObjects to export data first
- Copy to a backup index before deleting
- Add confirmation prompts in application code

structural_patterns.name: algolia-sdk-clear-objects
structural_patterns.reason: Algolia SDK clearObjects removes all records from an index.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The clearObjects SDK method removes all searchable records from an index while keeping configuration intact. Search results will be empty until re-indexing completes. This cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- Export objects using browseObjects first
- Use deleteObjects for targeted removal
- Test on a replica index before production

packs.name: Meilisearch
packs.description: Protects against destructive Meilisearch REST API operations like index deletion, document deletion, delete-batch, and API key removal.

structural_patterns.name: meili-curl-delete-document
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against /documents/{id} removes a document from Meilisearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a single document removes it from search results immediately. While less destructive than bulk operations, applications expecting this document will receive errors or empty results.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /indexes/{uid}/documents/{id}: Verify document first
- Export document content before deletion
- Use soft delete field in documents instead

structural_patterns.name: meili-curl-delete-documents
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against /documents removes documents from Meilisearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting all documents removes every record from the index while preserving settings and configuration. Search will return no results until re-indexing. This cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /indexes/{uid}/documents: Export documents first
- Create a snapshot or dump before deletion
- Use delete-batch for targeted removal instead

structural_patterns.name: meili-curl-delete-batch
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X POST to /documents/delete-batch deletes documents in bulk.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Batch delete removes multiple documents by their IDs in a single operation. This is irreversible and affects all documents matching the provided IDs. Verify the ID list carefully before executing.

Safer alternatives:
- GET documents by ID to verify content first
- Export matching documents before deletion
- Test with a small batch before processing all

structural_patterns.name: meili-curl-delete-key
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against /keys removes a Meilisearch API key.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API key immediately revokes access for all applications using it. Search and indexing operations will fail with authentication errors. The key cannot be recovered after deletion.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /keys: List and document keys before deletion
- Create replacement key before deleting old one
- Update applications with new key first

structural_patterns.name: meili-curl-delete-index
structural_patterns.reason: curl -X DELETE against /indexes/{uid} deletes a Meilisearch index.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Meilisearch index permanently removes all documents, settings, filterable attributes, and ranking rules. Search functionality for applications using this index will fail immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /indexes/{uid}: Export index settings first
- Create a dump with POST /dumps for backup
- Re-index from source data after verification

structural_patterns.name: meili-http-delete-document
structural_patterns.reason: http DELETE against /documents/{id} removes a document from Meilisearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a single document removes it from search results immediately. While less destructive than bulk operations, applications expecting this document will receive errors or empty results.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /indexes/{uid}/documents/{id}: Verify document first
- Export document content before deletion
- Use soft delete field in documents instead

structural_patterns.name: meili-http-delete-documents
structural_patterns.reason: http DELETE against /documents removes documents from Meilisearch.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting all documents removes every record from the index while preserving settings and configuration. Search will return no results until re-indexing. This cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /indexes/{uid}/documents: Export documents first
- Create a snapshot or dump before deletion
- Use delete-batch for targeted removal instead

structural_patterns.name: meili-http-delete-batch
structural_patterns.reason: http POST to /documents/delete-batch deletes documents in bulk.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Batch delete removes multiple documents by their IDs in a single operation. This is irreversible and affects all documents matching the provided IDs. Verify the ID list carefully before executing.

Safer alternatives:
- GET documents by ID to verify content first
- Export matching documents before deletion
- Test with a small batch before processing all

structural_patterns.name: meili-http-delete-key
structural_patterns.reason: http DELETE against /keys removes a Meilisearch API key.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API key immediately revokes access for all applications using it. Search and indexing operations will fail with authentication errors. The key cannot be recovered after deletion.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /keys: List and document keys before deletion
- Create replacement key before deleting old one
- Update applications with new key first

structural_patterns.name: meili-http-delete-index
structural_patterns.reason: http DELETE against /indexes/{uid} deletes a Meilisearch index.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Meilisearch index permanently removes all documents, settings, filterable attributes, and ranking rules. Search functionality for applications using this index will fail immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /indexes/{uid}: Export index settings first
- Create a dump with POST /dumps for backup
- Re-index from source data after verification
